EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow began to fall across central Kansas Friday night January 23rd and continued to fall through the 24th before ending early Sunday morning on the 25th.  Despite the long duration of snow most areas received modest amounts ranging from 5 to 8 inches. Air temperatures were unusually cold in the 0 to 5 above range making the snow very dry and powdery.  Winds were not strong with this event mainly staying in the 5 to 15 mph range so widespread blowing and drifting was not a widespread problem.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS report of 8.5 inches measured at KS-WS-14 6.9 miles southeast of Palmer in far southern Washington County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough positioned itself to support the development of a strong mid-level frontogenesis band. It took advantage of tremendous mid-level moisture and lift. Despite the initial more progressive nature of the system, it stalled out over SW Kansas creating some locally high amounts in excess of a foot of snow. The heavy snow brought widespread travel impacts. The snow started early on 1/9 and ended in the evening of the same day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported 5 inches of snow fallen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm impacted portions of far southeastern Kansas from January 23���25, 2026, bringing significant snow, ice, record-breaking cold to the region. The event, which peaked between Saturday, Jan 24, and Sunday, Jan 25, resulted in double-digit snowfall totals in some areas and widespread, dangerous travel conditions. Many locations receiving over 5 inches of snow. Some areas recorded 4 to 7 inches of snowfall with the highest amounts occurring along the Missouri State line as bands of heavy snow developed. Roads and highways were severely impacted, with partial to full coverage reported across the region, including US 69, making travel difficult or dangerous.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall began to push into the County during the late evening hours of January 23 as the first round of snow that would continue into January 25th. Moderate to heavy snowfall then occurred during through the day January 24 and into midday January 25th. Between 5-7 inches of snow were reported across the County, causing multiple schools and roads to close.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough positioned itself to support the development of a strong mid-level frontogenesis band. It took advantage of tremendous mid-level moisture and lift. Despite the initial more progressive nature of the system, it stalled out over SW Kansas creating some locally high amounts in excess of a foot of snow. The heavy snow brought widespread travel impacts. The snow started early on 1/9 and ended in the evening of the same day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report of 8 inches of snow fallen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Starting in the evening of the 23rd and continuing into the morning of the 24th, a winter storm moved across northwestern Kansas. Graham county received the highest snowfall amounts of up to 7 inches. Around midnight, the Hill City airport reported less than 0.5 mile visibility in blowing snow as winds were only around 10-15 MPH. Across the rest of the area, 1-5 inches of snowfall was reported by morning. The heavy snow caused reduced visibilities and slick roads, causing travel impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of 7 inches of snow 6 miles south-southeast of Hill City the morning of the 24th. Hill City ASOS recorded 0.5 mile visibilities overnight due to heavy snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm impacted portions of far southeastern Kansas from January 23���25, 2026, bringing significant snow, ice, record-breaking cold to the region. The event, which peaked between Saturday, Jan 24, and Sunday, Jan 25, resulted in double-digit snowfall totals in some areas and widespread, dangerous travel conditions. Many locations receiving over 5 inches of snow. Some areas recorded 4 to 7 inches of snowfall with the highest amounts occurring along the Missouri State line as bands of heavy snow developed. Roads and highways were severely impacted, with partial to full coverage reported across the region, including US 69, making travel difficult or dangerous.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall began to push into the County during the late evening hours of January 23 as the first round of snow that would continue into January 25th. Moderate to heavy snowfall then occurred during through the day January 24 and into midday January 25th. Between 3-6 inches of snow were reported across the County, causing multiple schools and roads to close.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds moved into central and south central Kansas starting in the evening hours of the 15th and continuing through the early evening of the 16th with a tightened gradient from a low pressure system. Generally winds had speeds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts up to 55 mph. The peak gust was at Russell Airport overnight with a measured gust of 63 mph. No damage was reported.

A prolonged winter storm affected the Southern Plains on January 23rd through the 25th. Central and eastern Kansas saw all snow for this event. It snowed for 40 straight hours in Wichita. Most locations across central, south central and southeast Kansas picked up between five to a little above nine inches; near Smolan in Saline County measured 9.1 inches for the event.||Extreme cold air spilled south across the Plains with this event.  Nine temperature records were either set or tied during the January 23rd through January 26th time frame. Both Russell and Salina saw low temperatures of -16 with widespread wind chills in the 10 to 20 below zero range. In Wichita, the homeless shelter provided housing for over 365 guests which is above average and near their capacity. There is one unconfirmed fatality of an unhoused individual who didn't seek shelter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on surrounding reports along with reports within the county, an estimated 6 to 7 inches of snow fell across Harvey County throughout the event. The highest reported snow total was 6.5 inches about two miles northeast of Newton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually warm and moist airmass, for January standards, moved into portions of southeast Kansas during the morning hours of January 8th. This provided an environment conducive for severe thunderstorms with 80+ mph winds being measured in Independence, Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Powerlines and power poles were down across the city. In addition, several outbuildings were damaged. A semi trailer was blown over as well. Entry door windows were broken and part of a roof was peeled off of a church.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged winter storm affected the Southern Plains on January 23rd through the 25th. Central and eastern Kansas saw all snow for this event. It snowed for 40 straight hours in Wichita. Most locations across central, south central and southeast Kansas picked up between five to a little above nine inches; near Smolan in Saline County measured 9.1 inches for the event.||Extreme cold air spilled south across the Plains with this event.  Nine temperature records were either set or tied during the January 23rd through January 26th time frame. Both Russell and Salina saw low temperatures of -16 with widespread wind chills in the 10 to 20 below zero range. In Wichita, the homeless shelter provided housing for over 365 guests which is above average and near their capacity. There is one unconfirmed fatality of an unhoused individual who didn't seek shelter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on surrounding reports and reports across the county, an estimated 6 to 8 inches fell across Allen County during the event. The highest report in the county was in Moran where 7.6 inches of snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through northwest Kansas on the evening of the 15th. Multiple wind gusts ranging from 58 mph - 63 mph were recorded. Multiple reports of blowing dust with less than 1/2 - 1/4 mile visibility reported. A wildfire was detected via satellite and confirmed with local emergency management.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire was detected via satellite at 3 ESE Wheeler and confirmed by an emergency manager. Currently awaiting further details.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow developed across western Kansas during the early morning hours of the 9th before ending during the late afternoon/early evening hours on the 9th. The highest snowfall amounts occurred across Greeley and Wichita counties with 3 inches reported in Tribune and 5-7 inches in Wichita county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wichita Emergency Manager estimated 5-7 inches of snow in the southern portion of Wichita county. 5 inches of snow was reported in Leoti at 1353 CST with snow still falling at the time of report.
